name: tock
documentation:
Tock
====

Tock stands for Theory Of Computing toolKit. It can simulate the
automata taught in standard theory of computation courses
(deterministic and nondeterministic finite automata, pushdown
automata, and Turing machines). It can also simulate many extensions,
like multiple stacks or tapes.

- [Project documentation](http://nd-cse-30151.github.io/tock/html/)

Installation
------------

Tock depends on the following:

-  Python 3.7 (required)
-  [GraphViz](http://www.graphviz.org) (to draw graphs)
-  [Jupyter](http://jupyter.org) (to
   view notebooks)
-  [pydot](https://pypi.org/project/pydot/) (to edit machines)   
-  [openpyxl](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/openpyxl) (to open Excel
   files)

Steps:

1. Run `pip install tock`.

2. Install [Jupyter](http://jupyter.org) by running `pip install
   jupyter` (or `conda install jupyter` if you use Anaconda).

3. Install [GraphViz](http://www.graphviz.org).

Copying
-------

This is open-source software under the MIT License. See `LICENSE.txt`
for more information.

modules.name: docs.source.conf
documentation: Configuration file for the Sphinx documentation builder.
This file only contains a selection of the most common options. For a full
list see the documentation:
https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/configuration.html
If extensions (or modules to document with autodoc) are in another directory,
add these directories to sys.path here. If the directory is relative to the
documentation root, use os.path.abspath to make it absolute, like shown here.
import os
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath('.'))

modules.name: tock.machines
documentation:
This module contains Tock's main data structure, the Machine class,
and associated classes and functions.

functions.name: tock.machines.FiniteAutomaton
documentation: A (deterministic or nondeterministic) finite automaton.
Define some standard automaton types.

functions.name: tock.machines.PushdownAutomaton
documentation: A (deterministic or nondeterministic) pushdown automaton.

functions.name: tock.machines.TuringMachine
documentation: A (deterministic or nondeterministic) Turing machine.

functions.name: tock.machines.from_transitions
documentation:
Create a `Machine` from transitions (in the same format returned by
`get_transitions`), trying to guess what kind of machine is intended.

- Store 0 is the state.
- Store 1 is the input.
- Whether a store is a TAPE is guessed based on whether the next
  field only ever has values of L or R.
- Whether the input is a STREAM is guessed based on the size
  of the right-hand sides of the transitions.

Not really meant to be used directly; used by `from_graph` and `from_table`.

classes.name: tock.machines.Store

methods.name: tock.machines.Store.match
documentation:
Returns true iff `self` (as a pattern) matches `other` (as a
store). Note that this is asymmetric: `other` is allowed
to have symbols that aren't found in `self`.

class_variables.name: tock.machines.Store.position
documentation: : The head position

A string together with a head position. It is used either as a
store of a Machine or as a pattern to be matched against a store of a
Machine.

Arguments:
    values: Sequence of Symbols
    position (int): The head position, between -1 and len(values)
    store (Store or str): Another Store to copy, or a str to convert to a Store

classes.name: tock.machines.Configuration

methods.name: tock.machines.Configuration.match
documentation:
Returns true iff self (as a pattern) matches other (as a
configuration). Note that this is asymmetric: other is allowed
to have symbols that aren't found in self.

class_variables.name: tock.machines.Configuration.stores
documentation: : A tuple of Stores

A configuration, which is essentially a tuple of `Stores`.

Arguments:
    stores: A tuple of Stores
    config (Configuration or str): Another Configuration to copy, or a str to convert to a Configuration

classes.name: tock.machines.Transition

methods.name: tock.machines.Transition.match
documentation: Returns True iff self can be applied to config.

class_variables.name: tock.machines.Transition.lhs
documentation: : left-hand side Configuration

class_variables.name: tock.machines.Transition.rhs
documentation: : right-hand side Configuration

A transition from one `Configuration` to another `Configuration`.

Arguments:
    lhs (Configuration): left-hand side
    rhs (Configuration): right-hand side
    transition (Transition or str): Another Transition to copy, or a str to convert to a Transition

classes.name: tock.machines.AlignedTransition
documentation:
A `Transition` that has an alignment between the lhs and rhs. These
are generated by `get_transitions` so that even if the number of
lhs and rhs stores changes, we can still keep track of their
relationship to the machine's stores.

If `t` is an `AlignedTransition`, `t[i]` is a `Transition`
containing the lhs and rhs just for store number `i`.

classes.name: tock.machines.Machine

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.num_stores
documentation: How many stores the Machine has.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.get_start_state
documentation: Return the start state.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.set_start_state
documentation:
Set the start configuration with `q` as the start state. The input
store will be initialized to the input string, and all other
stores will be initialized to the empty string.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.add_accept_state
documentation:
Add an accept configuration with `q` as the state. Any stores with
type STREAM must reach the end of the string in order to
accept. All other stores will not have any accepting
conditions.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.add_accept_states
documentation: Add a list of accept states (see `add_accept_state`).

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.get_accept_states
documentation: Return the set of accept states.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.states
documentation: The set of all possible states.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.add_transition
documentation:
Add a transition. The argument can either be a `Transition` or a
left-hand side and a right-hand side.

- If a store is a STREAM, there should not be an rhs; an empty
  rhs is automatically inserted.
- If a store is a TAPE, there should be two rhs's: a write
  (whose position is ignored) and a move (L or R).

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.add_transitions
documentation: Add a list of transitions (see `add_transition`).

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.get_transitions
documentation:
Return an iterator over all transitions, as AlignedTransitions.

- For any stores of type STREAM, the generated
  transitions do not have an rhs for the input.

- For any stores of type TAPE, the generated transitions will
  have an additional field to indicate a move (L or R).

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.edit
documentation: In a Jupyter notebook, returns a visual editor for this Machine.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.has_stack
documentation:
Tests whether store `s` is a stack, that is, it never moves from
position 0.
## Testing for different types of automata

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.has_cell
documentation:
Tests whether store `s` uses exactly one cell, and therefore can
take on only a finite number of states.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.has_input_stream
documentation:
Tests whether store `s` is an input stream, that is, it only
deletes and never moves from position 0.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.has_tape
documentation:
Tests whether store `s` is a tape, that is, it never inserts or
deletes symbols.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.has_readonly
documentation: Tests whether store `s` is read-only.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.is_finite
documentation: Tests whether machine is a finite automaton.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.is_pushdown
documentation: Tests whether machine is a pushdown automaton.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.is_turing
documentation: Tests whether machine is a Turing machine.

methods.name: tock.machines.Machine.is_deterministic
documentation: Tests whether machine is deterministic.

A machine (automaton) that accepts or rejects strings. It has one
or more stores, which can act as states, stacks, tapes, etc. When
it is run on a string, the stores are initialized to a start
configuration and rewritten by transitions until an accept
configuration is reached or no more rewrites are possible.

Arguments:

    store_types: A list of store types, one for each
      store. Allowed types are `BASE`, `STREAM`, and `TAPE`. See
      `add_transition` and `add_accept_state` for explanation of
      these types.
    state (int): The index of the store which is the state. The state
      is usually what distinguishes the start and accept
      configurations, and it is also treated specially when
      reading/writing machines.
    input (int): The index of the store which is the input store. When
      the machine is run on a string, the input store is initialized
      to the string.

modules.name: tock.runs
documentation:
This module contains functions for simulating machines on
strings. Normally, `run` is the only function one needs to use.

functions.name: tock.runs.run
documentation:
Runs machine `m` on string `w`, automatically selecting a search method.

Arguments:

    m (Machine):      The machine to run.
    w (String):       The string to run on.
    trace (bool):     Print the steps of the simulation to stdout.
    steps (int):      Maximum number of steps to run the simulation.
    show_stack (int): For PDAs, the maximum depth of the stack to show.

Returns:

    A Graph whose nodes are the configurations reachable from the
    start configuration (which has the attribute `start=True`). It has
    an accept configuration (attribute `accept=True`) iff `m` accepts
    `w`.

functions.name: tock.runs.run_bfs
documentation:
Runs machine `m` on string `w` using breadth-first search.

Arguments:

    m (Machine):  The machine to run.
    w (String):   The string to run on.
    trace (bool): Print the steps of the simulation to stdout.
    steps (int):  Maximum number of steps to run the simulation.

Returns:

    Same as `run`.

functions.name: tock.runs.run_pda
documentation:
Runs a nondeterministic pushdown automaton using a cubic-time
algorithm based on: Bernard Lang, "Deterministic techniques for
efficient non-deterministic parsers." doi:10.1007/3-540-06841-4_65

Arguments:

    m (Machine):       The machine to run, which must be a PDA.
    w (String):        The string to run on.
    stack (int):       Which store is the stack.
    trace (bool):      Print the steps of the simulation to stdout.
    show_stack (int):  The maximum depth of the stack to show.
    keep_nodes (bool): Keep all nodes that aren't PDA configurations

Returns:

    Same as `run`. Because stacks are truncated, the number of nodes
    in the returned graph may be less than the actual number of
    configurations (which may be infinite).

functions.name: tock.graphs.read_tgf
documentation:
Reads a file in Trivial Graph Format. Edge labels are read into the
`label` attribute.

functions.name: tock.graphs.from_graph
documentation: Converts a `Graph` to a `Machine`.

functions.name: tock.graphs.write_dot
documentation:
Writes a `Machine` or `Graph` to file named `filename` in GraphViz
(DOT) format.

functions.name: tock.graphs.to_graph
documentation: Converts a `Machine` to a `Graph`.

classes.name: tock.graphs.Graph

methods.name: tock.graphs.Graph.add_node
documentation: Add node `v` to graph with attributes `attrs`.

methods.name: tock.graphs.Graph.remove_node
documentation: Remove node `v`, as well as any edges incident to `v`.

methods.name: tock.graphs.Graph.add_edge
documentation: Add edge from `u` to `v` to graph with attributes `attrs`.

methods.name: tock.graphs.Graph.has_edge
documentation: Remove edge from `u` to `v`.

methods.name: tock.graphs.Graph.only_path
documentation:
Finds the only path from the start node. If there is more than one,
raises ValueError.

methods.name: tock.graphs.Graph.shortest_path
documentation:
Finds the shortest path from the start node to an accept node. If
there is more than one, chooses one arbitrarily.

methods.name: tock.graphs.Graph.has_path
documentation: Returns `True` iff there is a path from the start node to an accept node.

A directed graph. Both nodes and edges can have a `dict` of attributes.

Nodes can be any object that implements `__hash__` and `__eq__`.

If `g` is a `Graph` and `v` is a node, `v`'s attributes can be
accessed as `g.nodes[v]`. If `u` and `v` are nodes, edge (`u`,
`v`)'s attributes can be accessed as `g.edges[u][v]`.

functions.name: tock.syntax.parse_multiple
documentation:
Parse one or more comma-separated elements, each of which is parsed
using function f.

functions.name: tock.syntax.str_to_state
documentation: s is a string possibly preceded by > or @.

functions.name: tock.syntax.str_to_config
documentation: s is a comma-separated list of stores.

functions.name: tock.syntax.str_to_configs
tock.syntax.configs_to_str
documentation: Converts a set of Configurations to a str. The inverse of str_to_configs.

functions.name: tock.syntax.str_to_transition
documentation: s is a string of the form a,b or a,b->c,d

classes.name: tock.syntax.String

class_variables.name: tock.syntax.String.values
documentation: : A sequence of Symbols
A sequence of `Symbols` (not to be confused with `str`).
## Data structures that print more like in math books

functions.name: tock.regexps.str_to_regexp
documentation: ## Parser for regular expressions

functions.name: tock.regexps.from_regexp
documentation:
Convert a regular expression to a NFA.

Arguments:
    e (RegularExpression or str): the regular expression to convert.
    display_steps (bool): if True and if run inside a Jupyter notebook,
      displays all steps of the conversion.

functions.name: tock.regexps.to_regexp
documentation:
Convert a finite automaton to a regular expression.

Arguments:
    m (Machine): the automaton to convert, which must be a finite automaton.
    display_steps (bool): if True and if run inside a Jupyter notebook,
      displays all steps of the conversion.

classes.name: tock.regexps.RegularExpression

methods.name: tock.regexps.RegularExpression.from_str
documentation:
Constructs a `RegularExpression` from a `str`.

Regular expressions allow the following operations (precedence
from lowest to highest):

- Symbols
- Empty string (`&`)
- Empty set (`∅`)
- Union (`|`)
- Concatenation: Two concatenated symbols must be separated by
  a space. For example, `a b` is `a` concatenated with
  `b`, but `ab` is a single symbol.
- Kleene star (`*`)

A (abstract syntax tree of a) regular expression.

Arguments:

    op (str): Possible values are: 'union', 'concatenation', 'star', 'symbol'.
    args: tuple of `RegularExpression` or `Symbol` objects.

The empty string is represented as RegularExpression('concatenation', ()).

The empty set is represented as RegularExpression('union', ()).

functions.name: tock.grammars.from_grammar
documentation:
Convert a CFG to a PDA.

Arguments:

: g (Grammar): the grammar to convert, which must be a CFG.
  mode (str): selects which algorithm to use. Possible values are:

  > - `"topdown"`: nondeterministic top-down, as in Sipser (3e) Lemma 2.21.
  > - `"bottomup"`: nondeterministic bottom-up.
  > - `"ll1"`: LL(1) deterministic top-down.
  > - `"lr0"`: LR(0) deterministic bottom-up.
  > - `"lr1"`: LR(1) deterministic bottom-up.

Returns:

: Machine: a PDA equivalent to `g`.

functions.name: tock.grammars.from_cfg_ll1
documentation:
Convert a CFG to a PDA. If the CFG is LL(1), the resulting PDA will
be deterministic.

functions.name: tock.grammars.intersect_stack
documentation:
Given a PDA `p` and DFA `m`, construct a new PDA whose stack
language is the intersection of the stack language of `p`
(bottom-to-top) and the language of `m`.

This construction is the same as Hopcroft and Ullman (1e), page 254.

`p` can push and pop multiple symbols, and the resulting PDA does
push and pop multiple symbols.

functions.name: tock.grammars.lr_automaton
documentation: Construct the nondeterministic LR(k) automaton for CFG g.

functions.name: tock.grammars.from_cfg_lr0
documentation:
Convert a CFG to a PDA. If the CFG is LR(0), the resulting PDA
will be deterministic.

functions.name: tock.grammars.from_cfg_lr1
documentation:
Convert a CFG to a PDA. If the CFG is LR(1), the resulting PDA
will be deterministic.

functions.name: tock.grammars.pda_to_cfg
documentation:
Convert a PDA to a CFG, using the construction of Sipser (3e) Lemma 2.27.

Arguments:
  m (Machine): automaton to convert, which must be a PDA.

Returns:
  Grammar: A CFG equivalent to `m`.

classes.name: tock.grammars.Grammar

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.from_file
documentation:
Read a grammar from a file.

Arguments:

: filename (str): name of file to read from

Returns:

: Grammar: a CFG

The file should contain one rule per line, for example:

```
S -> a S b
S -> &
```

Currently the grammar must be a context-free grammar. The
nonterminal symbols are exactly those that appear on a
left-hand side. The left-hand side of the first rule is the
start symbol.

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.from_lines
documentation:
Read a grammar from a list of strings (see `from_file`).

Arguments:
    lines: a list of strings
Returns:
    Grammar: a CFG

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.set_start_nonterminal
documentation:
Set the start symbol to `x`. If `x` is not already a nonterminal,
it is added to the nonterminal alphabet.

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.add_nonterminal
documentation: Add `x` to the nonterminal alphabet.

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.add_rule
documentation:
Add rule with left-hand side `lhs` and right-hand side `rhs`,
where `lhs` and `rhs` are both Strings.

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.has_strict_start
documentation:
Returns True iff the start nonterminal does not appear in the rhs
of any rule. I don't know what the correct terminology for
this is.

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.is_noncontracting
documentation:
Returns True iff the grammar is *essentially* noncontracting, that
is, each rule is of the form α → β where one of the following is true:

- len(β) ≥ len(α)
- α = S, β = ε, and S does not occur on the rhs of any rule

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.is_contextsensitive
documentation:
Returns True iff the grammar is context-sensitive, that is, each
rule is of the form α A β → α B β where one of the following is true:

- A is a nonterminal and len(B) > 0
- A = S, α = β = B = ε, and S does not occur on the rhs of any rule

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.is_contextfree
documentation: Returns True iff the grammar is context-free.

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.is_leftlinear
documentation:
Returns True iff the grammar is left-linear, that is, it is context-free and
every rule is of the form A → B w or A → w where w contains only terminals.

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.is_rightlinear
documentation:
Returns True iff the grammar is left-linear, that is, it is context-free and
every rule is of the form A → w B or A → w where w contains only terminals.

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.remove_useless
documentation: Returns a new grammar containing just useful rules.

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.compute_nullable
documentation:
Compute, for every nonterminal and rhs suffix α,
whether α ⇒* ε.

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.compute_first
documentation:
Compute, for every terminal, nonterminal, and rhs suffix α, the set of
terminals b where α ⇒* b γ for some γ.

methods.name: tock.grammars.Grammar.compute_follow
documentation:
Compute, for every nonterminal A, the set of terminals b where 
S →* γ A b δ for some γ, δ.
A string-rewriting grammar.

methods.name: tock.grammars.DottedRule.__init__
documentation: change to DottedRule(lhs, rhs+lookahead, dotpos, lookpos)?
or: write lookahead like A b -> α b

class_variables.name: tock.grammars.DottedRule.rhs
documentation: includes lookahead symbols

class_variables.name: tock.grammars.DottedRule.dot
documentation: position of dot

class_variables.name: tock.grammars.DottedRule.end
documentation: length of true rhs

modules.name: setup
documentation: !/usr/bin/env python

modules.name: tock.operations
documentation: This module contains various operations on automata.

functions.name: tock.operations.determinize
documentation: Determinizes a finite automaton.

functions.name: tock.operations.equivalent
documentation: Test whether two DFAs are equivalent, using the Hopcroft-Karp algorithm.

functions.name: tock.operations.intersect
documentation:
Intersect two Machines.

Both machines should have a state as store 0 and input STREAM as
store 1. For example, 

- Both can be finite automata, in which case this is the standard
  product construction.

- One can be an NFA and the other a PDA, in which case the result
  is a PDA.

- The intersection of two PDAs would be a two-stack PDA.

functions.name: tock.operations.prefix
documentation:
Given a NFA `m`, construct a new NFA that accepts all prefixes of
strings accepted by `m`.

functions.name: tock.tables.read_csv
documentation:
Reads a CSV file containing a tabular description of a transition
function (see `from_table`).

functions.name: tock.tables.read_excel
documentation:
Reads an Excel file containing a tabular description of a
transition function (see `from_table`).

functions.name: tock.tables.to_table
documentation: Converts a `Machine` to a `Table`.

functions.name: tock.tables.write_csv
documentation: Writes `Machine` `m` to file named by `filename`.

classes.name: tock.tables.Table
documentation:
A simple class that just stores a list of lists of strings.

Arguments:
    rows: list of lists of strings
    num_header_rows (int): number of header rows
    num_header_cols (int): number of header columns
